1: The superscription links Psalm 59 to 1 Samuel 19:11–18: Saul sent agents to watch David's house through the night to kill him at dawn; Michal, David's wife, warned him and helped him escape through a window, putting a household idol in his bed as a decoy. The threat was specifically nocturnal — hence the "howling like dogs circling the city" imagery of vv. 6 and 14, which describes the watchers prowling around the house in the night. The personal and political situation is acute: the king himself has issued the kill order, so normal legal or social protections are unavailable. Psalm 59 is the third consecutive psalm using the al-tašḥēt miktam formula, grouping it with Pss 57–58 as a cluster of Davidic laments from the persecution period.
2: "Rescue me from those who do evil, and save me from men who want my blood" — ʾanšê dāmîm (men of blood, men who want bloodshed) is a characteristic Psalter phrase for those whose defining activity is lethal violence (Pss 5:6; 26:9; 55:23; 139:19). In the covenant legal system, shedding innocent blood was the gravest offense against the covenant community — blood pollution defiled the land itself and required judicial atonement (Num 35:33–34). To be threatened by "men of blood" was to be in a situation where the entire protective structure of covenant law had been subverted by the very ruler who should have enforced it.
3: "For see — they lie in ambush for my life; fierce men are gathering against me — not for any offense or sin of mine, LORD" — the protestation of innocence (bĕlōʾ-pešaʿ ûbĕlōʾ ḥaṭṭāʾtî) is a standard element of the individual lament and echoes the legal context of the Psalter's prayers. The speaker is not claiming sinlessness in a general moral sense but legal innocence in this specific case — there is no offense that would justify the attack. "Fierce men (ʿazzîm)" — the powerful and physically formidable who rely on their strength rather than law or justice.
4: "For no fault of mine they have mobilized against me. Arouse yourself, come to meet me, and look!" — ʿûrāh liqrāʾtî ûrʾēh (arouse yourself, come to meet me, and look). The "awakening" petition addresses what feels like divine inactivity or sleep — a bold appeal to the God who seems to have allowed the injustice to proceed unchecked. The same petition appears in Ps 35:23 ("Stir up yourself and awake for my defense!") and Ps 44:23–26 ("Awake, O Lord! Why are you sleeping?"). The prophetic tradition (Isa 51:9: "Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD!") also uses this language for dramatic urgency — the divine warrior called to action.
5: "You are the LORD God of hosts, the God of Israel — rouse yourself to punish all the nations; show no mercy to those who commit treason with evil intent" — the full title YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾel (LORD God of hosts, God of Israel) is the most complete military-theological title in the OT, invoking YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies and as the specific covenant God of Israel. The expansion to "all the nations (kol-haggôyim)" is striking — the immediate enemies are Saul's agents, yet the prayer addresses a cosmic scope. This move from particular threat to universal justice is characteristic of the Psalter's theology: the individual's crisis is embedded within the larger divine governance of all nations.
6: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling around the city" — the dog imagery in the ANE context is specifically derogatory. Dogs in the ancient world were primarily urban scavengers — ownerless packs that roamed city streets at night, fighting over refuse and corpses (1 Kgs 14:11; 21:23–24; 2 Kgs 9:10). To compare enemies to dogs was to portray them as dishonored, scavenging, unclean. The nocturnal timing ("each evening") fits the superscription's narrative of nighttime watchers surrounding the house. The circling motion — howling and prowling — describes the surveillance of Saul's agents waiting to close in at dawn.
7: "Listen — they pour out venom with their mouths; their lips are full of swords, for they think, 'Who is going to hear?'" — nābaʿ bĕpîhem (they pour out/bubble up with their mouths) — the verb describes the overflow of a spring or a bubbling up of language that cannot be contained. "Lips full of swords" repeats the weapon-mouth imagery of Ps 57:4 and Ps 55:21. The enemies' confidence — "who is going to hear?" (mî šōmēaʿ) — is the assumption that they are unobserved and unaccountable. This is the psalmist's most devastating indictment: the wicked sin because they believe God does not see or hear. The Psalter's response is that YHWH hears precisely what they say when they think no one is listening (Ps 94:7–11).
8: "But you, LORD, laugh at them; you scoff at all the nations" — the divine laughter (yiśḥaq ... yilʿag) here echoes Psalm 2:4, where YHWH laughs at the conspiracy of the nations against his anointed king. The laugh is not frivolous but expresses the sovereign's confident contempt for those who imagine their schemes can succeed against the divine governance. In the ANE, laughter was a weapon of superior power — the patron who could afford to laugh at a threat was demonstrating that the threat posed no real danger to his position. YHWH's laughter at the nations' plots is an implicit guarantee of the psalmist's safety.
9: "O my Strength, I will wait on you; for God is my fortress" — ʿuzzî (my strength) is used here as a divine title — the psalmist has internalized YHWH's character as his personal attribute. This divine-title pattern (my strength, my rock, my fortress, my shepherd, my light) is distinctive to the Psalter's personal theology: the abstract attributes of God become the first-person possession of the covenant worshipper. Miśgāb (fortress, high refuge, inaccessible stronghold) is frequently applied to YHWH in the Psalter (Pss 9:9; 18:2; 46:7, 11; 48:3; 62:2, 6; 94:22; 144:2) — the image of a natural high fortification that cannot be taken by assault.
10: "The God of my steadfast love will come ahead of me; God will let me look in triumph on my enemies" — ʾĕlōhê ḥasdî (God of my steadfast love — God who is the source of loyal-love toward me) personifies ḥesed as the defining attribute of the divine-human relationship. "Will come ahead of me" (yĕqaddĕmēnî) — qādam (to go before, to come in advance) is the language of divine guidance as a vanguard: God goes ahead of the speaker into the encounter with enemies. The scout or advance party provides intelligence and clears the path — YHWH as the one who prepares the ground before the covenant worshipper enters it (Exod 23:20; 33:14; Deut 31:8).
11: "Do not kill them, so my people will not forget; bring them down by your power and humble them, O Lord our shield!" — one of the most theologically distinctive petitions in the Psalter. The request is for restraint rather than destruction: preserve the enemies as living testimony to divine justice, so Israel will not forget what God did. This pedagogical use of judgment — enemies humbled rather than annihilated — functions as a didactic sign for the community. A similar logic appears in Exodus 9:16, where YHWH tells Pharaoh that he has been "raised up" specifically so that God's power might be displayed and his name proclaimed throughout all the earth. Obliteration of the wicked removes the witness; their humbling preserves it.
12: "For the sin of their mouths and the words of their lips, let them be ensnared in their own pride. For the cursing and deception that they utter" — speech is the vehicle of their sin: ḥaṭṭāʾt pîmô dĕbar śĕpātêmô (the sin of their mouth, the word of their lips). The prayer is that the very speech that is their weapon becomes the snare that catches them — the "own pride" (gĕʾônām) of confident self-assertion ensnares them. The OT principle that the wicked are caught by their own devices (Pss 7:15; 9:15; Prov 5:22; 11:5) is here applied specifically to linguistic pride and deception.
13: "Consume them in your wrath; consume them until they are no more, so that people may know that God rules over Jacob to the ends of the earth" — the universalizing conclusion: the purpose of divine judgment is knowledge (wĕyēdĕʿû), not merely satisfaction of justice. "God rules over Jacob to the ends of the earth" — the specific covenant identity (Jacob/Israel) is proclaimed universally; YHWH's lordship over his covenant people is the content of the knowledge that should reach the world's boundaries. This missional dimension of judgment runs through the Psalter: divine acts on behalf of Israel become the proclamation of YHWH's universal sovereignty (Pss 46:10; 98:2–3; Isa 49:6).
14: The dog refrain returns verbatim from v. 6: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling the city." The structural repetition of this refrain (vv. 6 and 14) creates a framing device for the psalm's middle section of trust and petition. The repetition also makes a theological point: the threat has not disappeared — the enemies are still prowling. But the speaker's inner state has changed from vulnerable pleading (vv. 1–7) to confident trust (vv. 9–13). The same external circumstance is now viewed from a different interior position.
15: "They roam around in search of food; they growl when they are not satisfied" — the nocturnal scavenging of the dog-enemies continues into the daybreak watch. Yĕnaʿû lĕʾĕkōl (they wander to eat) and yālînû (they howl/stay the night) — the word lûn can mean both "to spend the night" and "to grumble, mutter." The unsatisfied dogs growling through the night are a portrait of enemies whose schemes have not yielded the kill they sought. Their frustration — the speaker has escaped, they have not gotten their prey — is itself evidence of divine protection operating through Michal's warning (in the narrative setting of the superscription).
16: "But I will sing of your strength; I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning; for you have been my fortress and my place of refuge in my day of distress" — the dawn transition: the night of threat becomes the morning of praise. Ḥasdĕkā (your steadfast love/ḥesed) and ʿozzî (my strength) are the twin themes of the psalm's confidence. "In the morning" (babbōqer) is the classic rescue moment in the Psalter (Ps 30:5: "weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes in the morning"; Ps 46:5: "God will help her when morning dawns"). The divine pattern of night-terror and morning-rescue was liturgically formalized in the temple vigils, where worshippers waited through the night for the morning announcement of divine answer (Ps 130:5–6).
17: "O my Strength, I will sing praise to you; for God is my fortress, the God who shows me steadfast love" — the closing verse returns to the title ʿuzzî (my Strength) from v. 9, creating an envelope. The three attributes accumulated through the psalm — strength (ʿōz), fortress (miśgāb), and steadfast love (ḥesed) — form the theological summary of what YHWH has been in the speaker's crisis. The final characterization "who shows me steadfast love" (ʾĕlōhê ḥasdî, God of my ḥesed) personalizes the covenant relationship: this is not generic divine beneficence but the specific, directed loyal-love of the covenant God toward this particular person in this particular crisis.
